0f58cf66-5985-471a-9cff-969b12542ee5.title: Resolved: Security issue — 20/4/2026
0f58cf66-5985-471a-9cff-969b12542ee5.summary:
**What this document covers:**

This document details the resolution of an SSL certificate error (ERR_CERT_EXPIRED) that occurred on the company portal despite the certificate appearing valid. The issue was resolved by renewing the certificate through Let's Encrypt and restarting the web server.

**Key technical points:**

• Error presented as ERR_CERT_EXPIRED in browsers even though certificate appeared unexpired
• Initial troubleshooting included verifying certificate validity and system time without success
• Resolution involved running `certbot renew` on the server to obtain a new certificate
• Nginx web server was restarted using `systemctl restart nginx` to apply the new certificate
• New certificate valid for 90 days (standard Let's Encrypt duration)

**When to use this document:**

Reference this when users report ERR_CERT_EXPIRED errors on web portals where basic certificate validity checks don't reveal obvious expiration. This is particularly relevant for Let's Encrypt certificates that may have renewal issues or where the certificate chain needs to be refreshed even if the expiration date appears current.

0112a2d5-ceb4-4a6f-ac43-951d64b47655.title: Resolved: DevOps issue — 24/4/2026
0112a2d5-ceb4-4a6f-ac43-951d64b47655.summary:
**Document Summary: Jenkins Pipeline Deadlock Resolution**

What this document covers:
This document details the resolution of a critical Jenkins outage where all pipelines became stuck in a queued state due to a deadlock in the pipeline executor plugin, blocking all code deployments with 150+ builds pending.

Key technical points:
• Thread dump analysis revealed multiple executor threads in BLOCKED state caused by lock contention in the pipeline executor plugin
• Resolution required aborting all running and queued builds through both Jenkins UI and script console to release locked executors
• A controlled Jenkins service restart was necessary to clear the thread deadlock and reset executor state
• Root cause was traced to a recently updated pipeline-related plugin; fix involved rolling back to a stable version or applying a compatible update
• Recovery was validated by triggering test pipelines and confirming proper executor allocation and release

When to use this document:
Use this document when facing Jenkins pipeline deadlocks where builds remain indefinitely queued and executors fail to release locks. The outlined diagnostic and recovery steps apply to situations where thread dumps show BLOCKED executor threads and pipeline executor plugin issues are suspected.

64a5f895-4897-439e-8fb4-8e648d561b26.title: Resolved: Cloud issue — 4/5/2026
64a5f895-4897-439e-8fb4-8e648d561b26.summary:
**What this document covers:**
This document addresses AWS S3 cross-region replication failures between Mumbai and Singapore regions caused by IAM role permission errors, preventing 2TB of critical backup data from syncing and compromising disaster recovery capabilities.

**Key technical points:**
• Replication failure occurs despite bucket versioning being enabled, with AccessDenied errors on the IAM cross-region replication role
• Verify replication configuration using `aws s3api get-bucket-replication` to check role ARN, destination bucket, and rule settings
• Enable and monitor S3 replication metrics in CloudWatch, specifically OperationsFailedReplication counts to confirm failed object replications
• Root cause likely involves IAM role permissions, trust policies, or KMS encryption key access issues
• Resolution requires escalation to engineer with full IAM access to audit role configurations and review CloudTrail logs for specific permission denials

**When to use this document:**
Use this when experiencing S3 cross-region replication failures with permission-related errors that persist despite verifying bucket versioning and basic configuration. Apply when initial troubleshooting steps haven't resolved the issue and IAM/KMS permission auditing is needed to restore business continuity.

cf91d16f-2f20-4f24-8d6e-d75f9e2b1b30.title: AWS EC2 instance unreachable troubleshooting guide
cf91d16f-2f20-4f24-8d6e-d75f9e2b1b30.summary:
**AWS EC2 Instance Unreachable Troubleshooting Guide Summary**

This document provides systematic troubleshooting procedures for diagnosing and resolving AWS EC2 instances that become unreachable or unresponsive, covering both infrastructure and application-level failures.

Key technical points:

• Common symptoms include failed CloudWatch status checks (system or instance), HTTP 502/503/504 errors, SSH timeouts, and resource exhaustion (CPU, memory, or disk at 90-100%)

• Root causes fall into four categories: resource exhaustion (runaway processes, memory leaks, disk full), AWS hardware failures, security group/network ACL misconfigurations, and application crashes with failed restart policies

• Diagnostic workflow progresses through EC2 console status checks, CloudWatch metrics analysis, SSH connectivity testing, application log review, and load balancer health verification

• Primary resolutions include instance reboot (first attempt for unresponsive instances), stop/start cycle (fixes underlying hardware issues but changes public IP), and disk cleanup procedures using df, du, and journalctl commands

• Document specifies 30-minute response and 2-hour resolution SLA for critical production issues, with 3-5 incidents occurring monthly

Use this guide when EC2 instances become unreachable, applications stop responding, or CloudWatch alerts indicate failed status checks or resource saturation.

280eb4aa-813f-421d-ad18-34a2c1eb6e62.title: Netskope DNS Routing Troubleshooting Guide
280eb4aa-813f-421d-ad18-34a2c1eb6e62.summary:
**SUMMARY: Netskope DNS Routing Troubleshooting Guide**

This document explains how Netskope cloud security platform routes traffic through DNS interception and provides diagnostic steps for resolving connectivity issues when traffic fails to tunnel correctly.

**Key Technical Points:**

• Netskope intercepts DNS queries and resolves steered domains to gateway IPs in the 191.x.x.x range. Any other IP range (103.x, 52.x, 104.x, etc.) indicates traffic is bypassing the Netskope tunnel.

• When routing breaks, DNS resolves to public CDN IPs instead of Netskope gateways, causing Netskope's access policy to block direct connections and making applications appear inaccessible.

• Most common root cause is stale DNS cache retaining pre-Netskope IP resolutions. Other causes include client disconnection, domains missing from steering policy, split tunnel misconfigurations, and VPN conflicts.

• Primary diagnostic steps: verify client connection status, run DNS lookup (host/nslookup) to check if domain resolves to 191.x.x.x range, and perform mobile hotspot isolation test to rule out network-specific issues.

• VPN and Netskope conflicts occur when both are active simultaneously, with VPN potentially overriding Netskope's DNS interception through interface priority or routing tables.

**When to Use:**

Use this guide when troubleshooting application connectivity issues for users with Netskope client installed, particularly when applications are unreachable despite showing Connected status or when verifying correct traffic routing through Netskope POPs.

02036b08-a276-4b02-a170-b08de57dd207.title: DNST
02036b08-a276-4b02-a170-b08de57dd207.summary:
DNS TROUBLESHOOTING RUNBOOK SUMMARY

DOCUMENT COVERAGE
This runbook provides comprehensive operational guidance for diagnosing and resolving DNS failures within the company's dual-datacenter network infrastructure running BIND9. It covers internal DNS resolution failures, server health monitoring, misconfigurations, cache flushing, security incidents, and integration with Netskope DNS interception across Mumbai-01 and Mumbai-02 datacenters.

KEY TECHNICAL CONCEPTS
- The company operates Primary DNS (dns-server-prod-01, 10.0.4.1) and Secondary DNS (dns-server-prod-02, 10.0.4.2) running BIND9 v9.18.x on Ubuntu 22.04, with Google DNS (8.8.8.8/8.8.4.4) as forwarders for public domain resolution.
- Netskope client intercepts DNS queries on endpoints and steers specific domains to Netskope gateway (191.x.x.x), while non-steered domains pass through to corporate DNS servers for resolution.
- BIND9 handles both authoritative queries (for company.internal and internal view of company.com from zone files in /etc/bind/zones/) and recursive queries (for external domains, cached then forwarded to 8.8.8.8 if not found).
- DNS resolution flow: OS cache check → query to 10.0.4.1 → Netskope intercept/steering check → corporate DNS zone file lookup → forward to 8.8.8.8 if external → response cached locally. Timeout default is 5 seconds before failing over to secondary.
- Key BIND9 configuration files include /etc/bind/named.conf (master config), named.conf.options (forwarders, recursion, ACLs allowing queries only from 10.0.0.0/8), named.conf.local (zone definitions), and zone files like db.company.internal.
- DNS record types used: A records (hostname to IPv4), CNAME (aliases), PTR (reverse lookups in db.10 zone file), MX (mail routing), NS (nameserver delegation), and SOA (zone authority with serial numbers and refresh timers).

DIAGNOSTIC STEPS AND COMMANDS
The document references checking local DNS cache, verifying reachability to 10.0.4.1 and 10.0.4.2, SSH access for server-level diagnostics, confirming port 53 TCP/UDP is open, examining BIND9 zone files, and validating Netskope steering policy configuration.

RESOLUTION APPROACHES
Resolution methods include DNS cache flushing across platforms, correcting misconfigurations in BIND9 zone files and named.conf files, DNS server health recovery procedures, addressing split-horizon DNS issues, and handling security incidents like poisoning, amplification, and hijacking attacks.

WHEN TO USE THIS DOCUMENT
Use this runbook when troubleshooting application connectivity failures, websites not loading, email delivery issues, or VPN problems that may stem from DNS resolution failures. It's designed for network engineers, IT support, and NOC teams to independently diagnose DNS incidents without escalation.
